items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: What does the assumption 'ceteris paribus' mean in economic models?
items.correct: 'All other things equal' — when analysing the effect of one variable, all other variables are held constant. It is an analytical simplification, not a claim about reality.
items.misconception: Ceteris paribus means everything happens at the same time in real markets.
items.random_wrong: Ceteris paribus is a Latin name for the equilibrium price.
items.why: Real-world markets never hold all things equal — ceteris paribus is a thought-experiment device. Students treat it as a description of reality, missing why economists use it: to isolate cause and effect.

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.q: An oven in a bakery is which factor?
items.why: Correct answer: Capital. “Land” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Risk and organises others. “No risk” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many factors of production are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
